title: EOQ Calculator (Economic Order Quantity)
meta_description: Calculate your economic order quantity (EOQ). Enter annual demand, ordering cost, carrying rate, and unit cost to find the order size that minimises total cost.

inputs.label: Annual demand
inputs.help: Total units expected over the year.

inputs.label: Ordering cost
inputs.help: Fixed cost to raise and receive one order.

inputs.label: Carrying cost rate
inputs.help: Annual holding cost as a fraction of unit cost, e.g. 0.21 for 21%.

inputs.label: Unit cost
inputs.help: Purchase or production cost per unit.

result_cards.label: Economic order quantity
result_cards.description: Order size that minimises combined ordering and holding cost. Round to a practical pack or case multiple.

copy_blocks.intro: The economic order quantity is the order size that minimises the total of ordering cost and inventory holding cost. Order too little and you pay to order too often; order too much and you tie up cash in stock.
copy_blocks.how_it_works: EOQ balances the fixed cost of each order against the cost of carrying the resulting average inventory. It rises with demand and ordering cost, and falls as holding cost rises.
copy_blocks.formula_explanation: EOQ = sqrt( (2 x annual demand x ordering cost) / (carrying rate x unit cost) ). The carrying rate x unit cost is the annual cost to hold one unit.
copy_blocks.worked_example: With annual demand 20,000, ordering cost 125, carrying rate 21%, and unit cost 34: EOQ = sqrt((2 x 20,000 x 125) / (0.21 x 34)) = about 837 units.

faq.a: It sets a cost-efficient reorder quantity for stable, repeatably ordered items. Pair it with the reorder point to know both how much and when to order.
Skip it for just-in-time items, highly variable demand, or when big quantity discounts change the unit cost by order size.

faq.q: Should I order exactly the EOQ?
faq.a: No. Round to a practical case, pallet, or minimum-order multiple. EOQ is a target, not a rigid quantity.
